data.title: Badger Meter: Finally A Buy Following Q1 Earnings Tumble
data.description:
I've long held the view that Badger Meter (NYSE:BMI) was a great company selling at an unattractive price. Unfortunately, if you significantly overpay for a company, it can take years for the firm's underlying fundamentals to catch up with the share price even if the business keeps performing well.

However, I started to warm up on the firm a bit with my most recent update, upgrading BMI shares from a sell to a hold thanks to a more reasonable valuation in January. After an extended period trading with a sky-high valuation multiple, Badger Meter entered 2026 with a somewhat more grounded valuation point. And things have accelerated on that front over the subsequent three months.

The latest data point came with Badger Meter's soft Q1 earnings, which led to the stock getting clobbered in Friday's trading session. As I write this, shares are off 23% on the day. And in the bigger picture, Badger has given back nearly all its gains from the past five years:

With the stock now down more than 50% from the 52-week highs, enough is enough. Here's why I'm upgrading Badger to a buy.

Q1 Earnings: A Big Miss

On Friday, Badger released its Q1 earnings. Revenues of $202 million fell 9% year-over-year and missed by $29 million. That's a big whiff. Not surprisingly, given the size of the top-line shortfall, the firm's EPS of 93 cents also missed expectations badly, and slumped from the $1.30 reported in the same period of 2025.

The company attributed much of the revenue decline to the roll-off of certain lumpy projects which were completed in 2025 and thus made for a difficult comparative environment.

The miss was primarily driven by a 10% decline in Badger's utility water sales operation. However, flow instrumentation products also fell 4%, raising some concerns about the overall demand landscape as opposed to there simply being issues with utility water sales. The company's gross margin of 41.7%, while still elevated compared to its own historical average, also slipped a bit from the record-high 42.9% which it had reported in the same period of 2025.

One other note from the Q1 earnings release is that Badger announced a new acquisition. It will be acquiring UDlive, a U.K.-based sewer monitoring business, for $100 million. UDlive reported $22 million in revenues and was profitable in its most recent fiscal year. About 4.5x revenues for the acquisitions seems fair, that's not far above where Badger Meter shares have been trading in 2026, and it's well below BMI stock's historical median price-to-sales ratio over the past five years. $22 million of annual revenues isn't a game-changer for Badger, which already does more than $900 million in annual sales. But this sort of bolt-on acquisition, which fits nicely alongside Badger's existing U.S. sewer monitoring operation, should build shareholder value over time.

If these Q1 results are reflective of the future prospects of the business, then there would be no buying opportunity here. This was a really bad quarterly earnings report, there's no mincing words there.

However, management explained that this weakness was largely expected and a matter of timing rather than lack of demand. Here was CEO Kenneth C. Bockhorst's statement:

"Entering 2026, we expected project pacing dynamics to favor the back half of the year [...] Our first quarter results were consistent with that expectation, reflecting comparisons to the prior‑year period when several since completed projects were still in active deployment. Additionally, we experienced weaker than anticipated short-cycle order rates in the first quarter of 2026.

In our view, the year-over-year decline in revenue and associated operating leverage are related to variability in project timing and short-cycle customer order patterns and do not reflect a change in underlying demand, our competitive position, or the long-term market drivers for our business. We have a strong pipeline of awarded projects scheduled to launch in the second half of 2026, and our broader multi-year opportunity funnel remains robust."

Judging by the market reaction, folks seem to be fairly skeptical of this explanation that it's almost all a timing issue and tied to projects from last year which didn't recur.

However, the good news is that Bockhorst stated that Badger anticipates returning to sequential revenue growth next quarter, and that the company is projecting flat overall revenues for 2026 despite the decidedly rough start to the year. What this means is that, as investors, we will be able to confirm or reject management's explanation within the next couple of quarters.

Making The Bull Case

There is now some uncertainty here that has helped trigger the valuation reset. Specifically, Badger Meter was a steady compounding growth story in a high-quality industry.

Water is one of the more basic human needs, and there is little way that it would be disrupted the technological change. And, as people become wealthier, they are willing to spend more to improve the quality of their drinking water. That means plenty of basic water access and solutions investment in emerging markets along with increasing spending for dealing with "forever chemicals", lead, and other water contaminants in developed markets.

Meanwhile, water is also an integral component of innumerable industrial and manufacturing processes. As technology evolves, new highly-intensive water uses invariable emerge. Data centers are the latest such prominent example of that point.

Water also benefits from favorable investment dynamics. There aren't many pureplay water companies listed on U.S. stock exchanges. Most water businesses tend to either be privately-held or operate as subsidiaries within much larger industrial firms or conglomerates. Pureplay firms like Badger are relatively few in number. And there is high demand for water investments as the industry has a favorable long-term growth profile and it also attracts specific capital flows from green- and sustainability-focused funds.

Badger has long attracted investor interest, leading to its historically high valuation multiples. Water meters are a key part of the ecosystem, and smart meters in particular are driving more efficient usage and allowing customers to make more informed decisions. As companies deploy more AI and data analytics into their processes, this should drive further demand for metering upgrades to provide the infrastructure that feeds into these systems.

Badger enjoyed a huge uptick in demand coming out of the pandemic, with the 2021 infrastructure bill providing for a flood of water-focused spending.

I'd argue that the current choppiness around quarterly sales is probably reflective of how Badger Meter greatly benefitted from the macroeconomic landscape over the prior five years. Once a period of above-trend growth ends, investors often start to raise concerns about the core business model and sustainability of the growth strategy. But, in many cases, it can simply be that some reversion to the longer-term mean is in order after a period of unusually robust demand.

It's also worth noting that Badger has significant revenues from its software operations tied to water management. That's traditionally been viewed as a positive, as these high-margin SaaS revenues are more predictable and less cyclical than the water meter sales. That said, we've had a brutal sell-off in SaaS stocks in recent months, and it's possible that the market has discounted Badger's software revenues to some degree as well given current market conditions.

Badger Meter Stock Verdict

With this latest drop, Badger Meter has reached a point where I find the risk/reward outright attractive. After years of liking the company but having distaste for its share price, the stars have finally aligned for investors that want a piece of this story.

I'll grant that 2026 could continue to be choppy, especially if it takes another quarter or two before the anticipated demand recovery starts to take hold. That said, I believe the primary issue here was that the market was too comfortable with Badger given its favorable industry, strong demand profile, and accelerating growth picture. That led folks into complacency and assigning BMI stock as high as a 50x P/E multiple not too long ago.

That froth has now washed out of the market. I don't see the demand for smart meters or water management software going anywhere, though, and I expect buyers to step in as soon as revenues stabilize later this year.

data.title: Public Storage: Why We Took A Large Position In The 6.6% Yielding, A-Rated, Preferreds
data.description:
The Business

Headquartered in Texas, Public Storage (PSA) is in the business of self storage. This REIT owns, develops and operates self storage facilities that lease units on a month-to-month basis. At December 31, 2025, PSA operated 3,533 facilities (spanning 258 million net rentable square feet) across 40 states, with ownership interest in 3,171 of them. This business began operations in 1972 and was serving over 2 million customers by the end of 2025. The credit rating agencies are suitably impressed with this self storage juggernaut and so are we.

The Acquisition

The above numbers provide a snapshot of what was at the end of last year. December 31, 2026 is expected to look quite different, with PSA's acquisition of National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA). The REIT made its plans public on March 16 and quoting from the press release, NSA "owns more than 1,000 self-storage properties totaling 69M rentable square feet and 550,000 units across 37 U.S. states and Puerto Rico". NSA has around 66% of its properties focused in the Sunbelt states, as defined by the Company (the fine print in the snapshot below notes what NSA considers the Sunbelt).

This PSA-NSA deal is an "All-Stock" transaction, with a 0.14 exchange ratio.

...holders of NSA common shares and operating partnership units will receive 0.14 of a share of PSA common stock or partnership units for each NSA share or unit they own, representing a total consideration of $41.68 per share based on PSA’s closing share price on March 13, 2026.

Source: Press Release

The acquisition also includes the creation of a joint venture with the limited partners of NSA financed with around $2.2B in secured debt, including a $240M mezzanine loan from PSA. As far as assumption of liabilities is concerned, the acquirer will repay NSA's bank debt and senior unsecured notes. Additionally, PSA will assume NSA's mortgage debt, along with Series A/B/A-1 preferred securities. To that end, PSA has obtained financing commitments.

...arranged committed financing of $4.0 billion, to be provided by Goldman Sachs Bank USA and Wells Fargo Bank, National Association, comprised of a $2.0 billion corporate bridge loan and a $2.0 billion joint venture off-balance sheet bridge loan which will become permanent secured mortgage financing.

Source: Press Release

Having received unanimous approval from the board of directors, the deal is expected to close in the third quarter. This is, of course, contingent upon receiving shareholders consent. Upon completion of the transaction, the combined entity will have an enterprise value of close to $77 billion, with close to 4,600 facilities spanning 42 states.

While the common shares of both have been something we could never get behind, primarily on account of valuation, we recently did find one part of the capital structure attractive.

Preferred Securities

PSA has 14 preferred securities, starting from Series F and going all the way down to Series S. A comprehensive list of the alphabet soup has been provided below (prospectus for each can be found here).

As we can see, Series F is second to only Series H in terms of its coupon rate. However, the current yield is what really matters for prospective investors. Recently, we found a few of the 14 quite interesting and divvied up a considerable chunk of our investment dollars between the two.

1) Public Storage PFD SHS F 5.15% (PSA.PR.F)

This Series pays an annual dividend of $1.2875, divided into quarterly instalments of 32.19 cents each. The payments are made on March 31, June 30, September 30 and December 31. They have been redeemable (at $25 plus accrued dividends) since June 2, 2022, but considering the current interest rate, the chance of that are slightly less than zero.

2) Public Storage 4% DEP SHS PFD P (PSA.PR.P)

This Series pays an annual dividend of $1, divided into quarterly instalments of 25 cents each. The payments are made on March 31, June 30, September 30 and December 31. They will be redeemable (at $25 plus accrued dividends) at or after June 16, 2026. If Series F is not being redeemed, Series P, with the lower coupon is definitely not going anywhere, anytime soon.

Moody's gave the preferred securities of PSA a A3 rating back in 2021 and that has not changed.

Moody's Investors Service, ("Moody's") has affirmed the ratings of Public Storage, including its A2 senior unsecured and A3 preferred stock ratings. The rating outlook remains stable.

Source: Yahoo Finance

We picked up PSA.PR.F at $19.65 and PSA.PR.P at $15.20. Both had an yield of 6.6% at the time. The price has increased a bit since then, but not too much. The current yield for both is around 6.30%. We also picked up multiple other series for our clients and in general got about 6.6% yield at the time. The advantage of splitting is that these are thinly traded and it is hard to buy substantial amounts of one, without driving up the price. This was shared within our Marketplace service at the time.

Verdict

PSA pays out $3 in quarterly distributions on its common shares, which based on the current market price of around $302, gives us an yield of close to 4%.

We got over 6.5% for holding the preferred securities of the same A2 rated company, rated just a notch below at A3. Preferred securities carry tons of duration so if we are to compare, we have to look at ultra-long duration bond funds, and of course preferred share funds. Starting off let's look at how that yield stacks up to a fund like iShares 10+ Year Investment Grade Corp Bond ETF (IGLB) that has an yield of 5.5% and holds investment grade corporate bonds. The average rating of the portfolio holdings is lower than the preferred securities of PSA.

You just come out ahead in the game with PSA, and with less risk. We are not even getting into the advantage of dividends relative to interest. Of course there are preferred funds like iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). Even this yields less than PSA preferreds, while going down significantly in credit quality.

In fact, as we have shown, this fund routinely buys and sells with zero sensitivity to price in the last 30 minutes of the last trading day of the month and creates some serious drag as a result. While the preferred shares are higher, they still remain more appealing than almost all comparable ones. We rate them a buy if you get them at 6.5% or higher yield, but they might still make sense for a switch from some investments you currently hold.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: lululemon: New Styles Are Driving Full-Price Sales
data.description:
In a surprising turn for the markets since the Iran conflict began, the S&P 500 has found footing at fresh new all-time highs. With the velocity of the rebound in the past few weeks, many investors are now asking, are there still buyable pockets left in the market?

I still find vast appeal in rebound plays, particularly in the software and retail stocks that have fallen hard this year. Within retail, I believe lululemon athletica inc. (LULU), the activewear company that has been struggling with maintaining share in the United States, is appealing after a ~20% decline since the start of the year.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on lululemon in January, when the stock was trading just north of $200 per share. While the timing on my buy call was premature, my conviction in the stock remains high, especially after a favorable recent Q4 earnings print that demonstrates the company understands what its issues are and has a plan in place to resolve performance issues. I’m reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

To me, these are the core reasons to be long on lululemon:

  • Reigniting product innovation to return to full-price sales. One of lululemon’s core issues has been stale product, which has led to clearance sales of slow-moving seasonal inventory that damaged not only the company’s gross margins but also the prestige of its brand. The company has released new high-performance training materials, including a new men’s/women’s lineup called Unrestricted Power using new lightweight stretchy materials.
  • Fierce international growth. Even though lululemon’s U.S. sales have been stagnant, the company has been achieving accelerating performance overseas. International is growing at a 20%+ clip, powered by new markets including South Korea.
  • Relatively high gross margins. Though lululemon has been battered by tariffs, the company still maintains a rich mid-50s gross margin profile that enables economies of scale. The potential rollback of tariffs is a further gross margin opportunity for lululemon, as is its improvement in full-price sales.

The core appeal to investing in lululemon now, of course, is valuation. For the current year, FY26 (ending January 2027), the company has guided to 2-4% revenue growth and $12.10-$12.30 in pro forma EPS growth.

And for next year, FY27, Street estimates point to an improvement to 5% revenue growth and $13.38 in EPS, which is 10% growth versus this year’s earnings guidance midpoint. Valuations are further supported by lululemon’s healthy balance sheet, which has $1.81 billion of cash unencumbered by debt (9.5% of the company’s current market value).

At current share prices near $168, this positions lululemon’s valuation multiples at:

  • 13.8x FY26 P/E, or 12.5x ex-cash.
  • 12.6x FY27 P/E, or 11.3x ex-cash.

There’s no doubt that lululemon has a hill to climb ahead of it when it comes to execution. It’s needless to say that reviving brand momentum is tough, especially now in the wake of a weaker consumer spending environment plus the presence of ascendant competition from new brands. That said, risks are priced in with the stock trading at steep discounts to the S&P 500. We also find that lululemon’s discount versus similarly struggling sportswear titan Nike (NKE) is particularly compelling:

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 Recap

Let’s now go through lululemon’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

lululemon's revenue grew just 1% y/y to $3.64 billion, which still beat Wall Street’s expectations of $3.58 billion (-1% y/y) by a two-point margin. We note, however, that the company faced a tough comp versus a 14-week quarter in the prior-year Q4.

The better measure of the company’s organic growth, as usual, is its comp sales trends, which measure revenue growth from existing store locations, excluding new store openings. Comp sales trends by market are shown below:

There’s good news here on all fronts. Specifically in North America, the company achieved -2% in constant currency comp sales. While there’s a long way to go to full recovery, this is a step change relative to a much deeper -5% comp sales slide in Q3.

Management attributed fresh new products as having a lift on sales during the critical holiday quarter. Also, the company notes that as new styles have made their way into stores, full-price sales in Q1 in the North American market have improved relative to Q4, pointing to recovery in U.S. comps in the year ahead.

Per co-CEO Andre Maestrini’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Looking at 2026, a primary goal in the North America is returning the business to healthier levels of full-price sales after seeing a higher markdown penetration in 2025. We are already seeing better full-price sell-through in Q1 relative to Q4, and we are targeting further improvement as we move through the year, driven by increased product newness, innovation and operating discipline […]

And third, we are increasing new style penetration across our assortment. Meghan already spoke to our product creation pillar. So I'll just add that in North America, with our new spring merchandise that is already hitting our stores and website, we have increased our new style penetration to approximately 35%. We know that our guests are looking for new styles and product from us. And when we deliver them effectively, we see strong response. In addition to what Meghan mentioned, other examples of successful new styles include EasyFive and the Groove Wide-Leg.

Sales strength was even more pronounced overseas. Q4 comp sales in international markets skyrocketed 14% y/y, doubling versus a 7% growth pace in Q3. Overseas sales were anchored by mainland China, which grew 21% in constant currency and improved versus 19% growth in Q3.

We still need to keep an eye on lululemon’s margins and profitability. Gross margin of 54.9% declined -550bps y/y, with the company citing a 520bps headwind from tariffs plus 130bps of unfavorably higher markdowns (we’ll need to wait through FY26 to see more favorable full-price sales turning into a tailwind for margins versus FY25’s deep discounting).

Operating margins of 22.3%, meanwhile, fell 670 bps y/y as the company also invested 1 percentage point of revenue more into SG&A expenses on a y/y basis, particularly to build new buzz and brand awareness for the company’s new product lines. Despite this, lululemon’s pro forma EPS of $5.01 still beat Street expectations of $4.78 with 5% upside.

Risks And Key Takeaways

When investing in a turnaround name like lululemon, investors need our “eyes wide open” on the plethora of risks ahead. Many retail management teams have cited a very promotional environment amid a tough macro exiting the holiday season: lululemon may be forced to continue discounting, not just to clear out old stock, but to keep up against peers. We're also disappointed by the company's deleverage on opex costs: in my view, in order for lululemon to really drive a recovery in enthusiasm, it needs to embark on a bigger cost-cutting plan in favor of utilizing AI instead of headcount.

That said, we find risks to be fully priced in at a ~13x forward P/E, on top of a healthy cash reserve. Stay long here.

data.title: GSY: Still Carries FX Risks, Spot Yields Looking To Fall
data.description:
The Invesco Ultra Short Duration ETF (GSY) is an ETF that helps investors back away from any sort of duration conviction, since the index essentially pays out a yield that compensates investors according to spot rate assumptions. While we are still somewhat bearish on duration as in our previous coverage, in this case because the structural and conflict risks remain elevated in our view, short term duration is going to lose some yield on the latest developments in the Middle East, namely all-front ceasefire, continued negotiations, and momentary reopening, without conflict, of the Strait of Hormuz. If this ceasefire endures (which is not a given), we don't have to worry about supply crunch anymore which is going to be important for allowing the Fed to continue with cuts, which they couldn't contemplate while the War went on. But this isn't great for spot yields. Of course, there is no capital gain threat or benefit from changing spot assumptions because there is no duration here. The point is, with GSY effectively equal to cash with a yield in terms of portfolio risk characteristics, we'd move into some select equities, also because GSY isn't going to get a positive capital gains effect from falling YTMs from lower credit spreads and benchmark rate assumptions. Also, for foreign investors, the USD outlook is getting a bit gloomier with maybe the return of trading factors like the debasement trade coming back. Finally, GSY has a higher expense ratio than the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF (FLOT), which accomplishes basically the same thing, though actually better because it truly has negligible duration while GSY has 0.58 years. We'd always pick FLOT over GSY.

GSY Breakdown

Expense ratio is 0.22% relative to the FLOT's 0.15%. Both are basically spot duration bets. Both have mostly single-A rated bonds, lots of commercial papers and things like that - one of the biggest markets for very short term credit, in this case corporate credit. The small credit spreads are also probably falling now on reduced macroeconomic risks related to the War, as speculated for the moment. Of course, the War could reignite and we'd be back to a week ago in terms of market dynamics.

The GSY is attractive when spot rates are remaining high. When they fall, lacking much duration at all at 0.58 years, there is very little effect on price but the incentive to keep cash and the GSY's yield falls. Usually, this will coincide with some sort of positive movement in the market, unless the declines in rates are to tackle a recession.

Bear Case

In this case, the pause of the Iran War and associated risk reduction to shipping through the Strait is a seriously positive thing for the broader market and equities, and warrants moving away from cash, particularly in equities that are lagging the recovery but shouldn't be. The pause the Iran War is already having an effect on 1 year rates, reflecting that the Fed rationale for holding rates and not continuing cuts is becoming moot. The Fed needs to be very cautious when the spectre of reinflation shows itself, particularly as actual inflation risks, and indeed has in fact climbed, over the upper 3% band. Consumer sentiment and awareness of inflation was also at alarming levels that risked wage-price spiral effects through expectations mechanisms. Particularly with the momentary opening of the Strait, where terminals can get filled again, speculation and upward pressure on oil prices can abate a bit. But as such, yield will start trending down. Moreover, there is considerable opportunity cost in being out of the market at the moment, particularly where it is possible to find bets that aren't running up quite as much yet, or were more deeply discounted and still below pre-Iran war levels.

The lessening macro risks is probably also reducing the spot yield from the credit risk to the GSY, but without the duration benefit of lower YTMs from lower benchmark rates and credit spreads to drive a price increase in GSY.

For foreign investors, it's also an opportunity to get away from the USD, which may go back to being a victim of the debasement trade, particularly with some perceiving the US as having projected some amount of weakness in how sensitive the administration was to the electoral risks of the Strait's closure. But more concretely, the USD suffers when the yield curve translates down as the compensation for holding dollar falls and reduces dollars demanded by allocators.

On a specific and comparable note, the higher expense ratio compared to FLOT means there is a strictly better option for addressing a low duration exposure.

Bull Case

On the other hand, having cash in an uncertain moment, even if yields are falling 5 bps or so on an annualised basis only, still affords optionality to respond to possible new dips. The pause in the War could be broken by unexpected military action and the ceasefire may not foster a successful peace deal process. Also, it could take considerable time before logistics sort themselves out. Even the current oil futures curve doesn't see normalisation below $80 per barrel happening until July.

Bottom Line

The ceasefire has deservedly put the possibility of a more sustained peace on the table, with the sides coming together now for a second time after a first attempt fell through. Markets consequently are rallying on the lesser risks and lower cost of capital prospects, which is essential for broader markets with high multiples, large horizon values and related discount factor sensitivities. While it's not about timing markets, there remain picks in more obscure corners that are either still discounted relative to recent history, or are rallying less than they should be. With lower yields coming on cash, and also for foreign investors the FX risk of staying in a USD-denominated instrument with older trading dynamics possibly returning assuming a peace deal is brokered, GSY should be lower priority right now. It won't benefit in terms of capital appreciation either from lower YTMs, both from lessening macro risks for the moment and also from higher probability of rate cuts. Of course, all this is assuming the ceasefire endures into a peace deal, otherwise having cash would be very opportune.

data.title: Telecom Sector May Be Well-Positioned To Handle Inflation And AI Risks
data.description:
Though there are concerns around wireless pricing, Nicholas Clarke, Associate for Investment Grade Credit Research with TD Asset Management, says the telecom sector may be poised to handle risks from inflation and AI. He joins MoneyTalk for a look at the telecom space from a bond perspective.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: The Canadian telecom sector has seen some volatile trading recently amid fears of renewed price war in wireless. But how does the health of these companies look from a bond perspective? Joining us now to discuss is Nick Clarke, Associate for Investment Grade Credit Research with TD Asset Management.

Nick, great to have you here. We talk a lot about the telecom sector from an equity perspective on the show, not often from a bond perspective. How should we be thinking about things?

Nicholas Clarke: Yeah, great to be here. Thanks, Greg, for having me on.

I think that the big thing is you can start with maybe a similarity. And that is investors look for these companies for income exposure for their portfolios. So, on the equity side, that would be a dividend. On the bond side, that would be a coupon or interest payment.

Now, there are differences, of course, in terms of how you get that exposure. And one of the big ones is the risk to that income changing. With fixed income or with bonds, that's a contractual obligation, where dividends are a little bit more earnings-dependent.

In terms of the differences, obviously, when you look at stocks, typically you're thinking price appreciation might be part of the equation here in terms of why I might make an investment. And with bonds, that's normally less so. But we do think in this sector right now, there is potential for bonds to actually increase in value in the next few years. And that's because a lot of these companies have actually taken on a lot of debt, and they're starting to pay it down. And all else being equal, as that debt decreases, the value of the bonds that remain outstanding goes up.

Greg Bonnell: Okay, so nice overview of the telecom space in general. Let's dig into some specific names now. We're going to talk about Rogers. It's been working to bring down its debt levels.

Nicholas Clarke: Yeah, so Rogers increased debt levels earlier in the 2020s in its takeover of Shaw Communications. It's had a little bit longer of a time in terms of getting that debt back down. One of the key strategies that they're working on right now is being able to monetize some of their sports assets. We all know about the Blue Jays-- they own 100% of the Blue Jays. They own 75% now of the Maple Leafs, of TFC, and some real estate assets as well associated with those teams.

What they're trying to do is a two-step process, really, which is to increase the ownership of those teams and those assets to 100%, probably looking at combining them all into one sports entity, and then selling down a minority interest in that to be able to pay down some debt and restore leverage to more sustainable levels.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about Telus. From an equity perspective, people have been, I think, concerned about the stock. I think it's fair to say it's pulled back quite dramatically since the fall. How does it look from a bond perspective?

Nicholas Clarke: Well, that's actually a good context, I think, because with Telus, the dividend yield is quite high. I believe it's close to double what Rogers and Bell is right now. And the question is, how sustainable is that dividend? And they do have a major leadership change coming up at the company. I believe there's a new CEO starting in July of this summer.

So, the question is, is there going to be an opportunity for the company, for management to be able to make a change to dividend policy at that point? And I think a lot of investors still are thinking about roughly a year ago, when Bell cut their dividend in half or maybe even by a little bit more.

So there's definitely some worries about that. But it's a good example of something that would be probably negative for an equity investor but positive for a bond investor in the sense that that would help the company be able to retain more cash flow and use that to reduce leverage.

Greg Bonnell: It will be interesting to see when Victor Dodig takes the big chair, which he-- former CIBC CEO-- what happens with that dividend. But a lot of people are watching that carefully.

Let's talk about those concerns around wireless pricing. I think there were some people on our side of the industry saying, I think the price war has subsided and we can move past this, and now we're afraid of it again.

Nicholas Clarke: Yeah, I think that a lot of people were kind of getting cautiously optimistic on what the sector was looking like going into the holiday season in 2025-- so, think late November, early December. But the first quarter of this year hasn't been quite as positive as people, I think, were hoping, in terms of price rationality or rational price behavior among the big players.

So, what does that mean? Typically, Q1 is a low-load quarter, as they say, which means that there's not as many subscribers being added to these networks as there are in other parts of the year. I believe if you look back 10 years, it's roughly 10% of the annual net adds that come onto these networks, is in the first quarter.

So, we have historical precedent to say that maybe there's not a lot of volume being added at these lower advertised prices. But we're going to have to see as earnings kick off next week, really, what the impact is going to be on ARPU, which is a metric that the industry looks at in terms of the actual price being paid for these new subscribers.

Greg Bonnell: Average revenue per user, right?

Nick Clarke: That's right.

Greg Bonnell: Something I remember from my old days when I used to cover the telecoms a little more carefully.

So, we covered a lot of ground here. With all that in mind, what's the potential opportunity right now for investors?

Nicholas Clarke: So I think there's maybe three things to keep in mind here. Obviously, what's going on in the Middle East is going to be something that is top of mind for most investors. Is this going to cause some kind of energy-related inflation shock? And will that slow down growth? Everyone's trying to figure that out as that situation remains fluid.

So, when we look at these companies, in terms of consumer spending, you think about certain things that families might say-- "Let's hold off on that for a little bit." And I think sitting down with your family at the kitchen table and saying, "Let's take a few months off internet" is probably pretty low on the list. So we look at it from that perspective, in terms of being what we call an inelastic business model in terms of the demand for it. So, we think that that's attractive against this macro backdrop.

And then number two would be, another kind of headline-grabber recently that's been top of mind has been what's going on with AI disruption and private credit specifically. And will that be some kind of contagion that will broaden-out into the market? And we see the sector as pretty safe from that in terms of AI disruption. If anything, we think that it could benefit from it in the longer term. So, we're not too concerned about AI as it pertains to the valuations here.

And then number three would just be, I think, the all-in yields. It's really important to recognize they're still quite high on an absolute basis. And when you compare them to the dividend yields that are being offered, for example, on Rogers or Bell, the bond yields are actually really close to that level right now. And that's not been the case in the past in some years.

So, we'll see what happens with Telus-- if that converges as well the way it has for Bell. But I think those three things are quite attractive. And as we look forward here, we have a number of macro events that I think are going to cloud the outlook for investors in the next little while. And we think there's a safety case to be made, or a defensive case to be made, for exposure to these bonds in this sector.

Original Post

data.title: Carlyle Secured Lending: Portfolio, Yield, And Capital Structure Review
data.description:
Managed by The Carlyle Group, оne of the lаrgest global investment manаgers, Carlyle Secured Lending Inc. (CGBD) has аccess to a brоad nеtwork of dеals and dеep crеdit expеrtise. In this аnаlysis, we exаmine the portfоlio structurе, finаncials, and the rеlative attrаctiveness of the cоmmon stock vеrsus the cоrporate bоnds as of the end of 2025. And if you are interested in our last article for the company, you can read it here.

CGBD In Details

CGBD has 165 holdings. The biggest part of it is in First Lien debt - close to 84%, followed by 6% in Equity Investments, 6% in Investment Funds, and 4% in Second Lien Debt. When we look at the industry exposure, the largest percentage is 18% in "Healthcare and Pharmaceuticals", followed by "Software" at 11%.

The total assets of CGBD as of December 31, 2025, are close to $2.79 billion. Close to 2.7% are in cash and cash equivalents, and around 87% are the total investments at fair value of the total assets:

The total debt is close to $1.54 billion. 100% of the balance sheet leverage of CGBD is with a floating rate. In the next picture, you can see the debt and the debt maturities of the company:

The company's 3-year average annual total return on NAV is close to 9.04%, and the 5-year is around 11.25%. Both numbers are higher than the peer groups.

Historical Premium/Discount

The next chart is a 10-year premium-discount of CGBD. We see its lowest point was during the COVID pandemic. Currently, the discount of the common stock is also higher - close to 28% - and it is at its lowest point.

We see that the discount is more than twice the 5-year average of 12.97%:

Asset Yield, Return On Equity, And Expenses

The annual total investment income is close to $255.6 million. The operating expenses are around $65 million. The operating expenses of CGBD are close to 25.4% of the total investment income. With assets of around $2.79 billion, the gross assets yield is close to 9.16%. The non-leverage gross expense ratio is around 2.32%.

The average loss of yield is close to 0.95%, and for the last 3 years, the average loss/total income is around 9%. When we include all of these, the expected ROE for the common stock is around 6.7%, but because the common stock is at a nearly 28% discount, the discount-adjusted ROE increases to around 9.31%. The number is, as expected, close to the 3-year annualized average total return of the company.

Let's look at the BDC's bonds:

The average yield to maturity of the group is close to 6%. To make the proper comparison with the common stock, we add leverage to the average OTC bond and consider a hypothetical capital structure with 200 total assets and 100 net assets. The expected ROE is close to 8.5%, which is less than one point from the expected ROE of CGBD, and in this case, we will continue to prefer the less risky bond. But this is our opinion - you can always choose from both or none.

Conclusion

CGBD is a well-divеrsified BDC with a high proportion of sеnior sеcured debt and a histоrical performance that outperforms its pеer group. The currеnt discоunt of arоund 28% seеms attrаctive at first glаnce. Hоwever, after adjusting, the expected ROE of the common stock excеeds that of the bоnds by less than one percentage point. Given this difference, the additional risk inherent in equity capital is difficult to justify based solely on the discount.

data.title: Citizens Financial Group: Margin Expansion Continues To Drive Strong Growth
data.description:
A wider net interest margin, more progress on the rollout of its private bank, and a benign credit backdrop continue to kick in nicely for Citizens Financial Group (CFG), with revenue and earnings both growing double-digits year-over-year in the first quarter of 2026.

Rating the stock "Buy" last December, I liked the fact that much of this growth was essentially baked into Citizens' balance sheet. So long as interest rates remain relatively constructive, the natural roll-off of its low-yielding fixed-rate assets will be a multi-year tailwind to its earnings, while the buildout of its nascent private bank, previously a drag, had also begun contributing positively to the bottom line. With Citizens' earnings forecast to grow strongly on the back of these drivers, a then-multiple of 14.5x consensus 2025 EPS looked like a bargain.

These shares have returned around 16% since then, around 8 points better than the wider regional banking space. With that still leaving Citizens on a mid-teens multiple of trailing EPS, I still think the market is undervaluing its growth prospects. Accordingly, I rate shares "Buy".

NIM Expansion

Citizens earned a net profit of $1.13 per share last quarter, up nearly 50% year-on-year on the back of 12% growth in revenue. A widening net interest margin ("NIM") has been a major driver of this growth, and this has been a key plank of my investment case for the bank. While it isn't the only institution to have benefited from a healthier NIM, Citizens' dynamics have been exacerbated by its significant portfolio of receive-fixed swaps. These derivatives, which were taken out several years ago, essentially fixed a chunk of its floating-rate loans at relatively low yields, weighing on its NIM. The roll-off of these swaps now represents a multi-year tailwind.

Consistent with this, Citizens' NIM expanded by another 7 basis points last quarter, 5 basis points of which came from the impact of its receiver swaps. 0.07% may not sound like much, to which I would offer three counter points. Firstly, that's just the sequential gain. Year over year, Citizens' NIM grew 24 basis points last quarter. Secondly, Citizens has around $200 billion in interest-earning assets on its balance sheet. All else equal, an extra 0.07% of margin adds $140 million to annual interest revenue – a meaningful amount for a bank that earned $1.7 billion in net profits last year.

That leads me nicely to the third and most important point. As I have mentioned past pieces, NIM-driven revenue growth doesn't come with any incremental costs. It largely drops right down to pre-tax income, explaining why its earnings growth has been so rapid.

Per above, the tailwind from its swaps should continue beyond 2026, and management is guiding to a 3.30-3.50% NIM by Q4 2027. Given $200 billion in earning assets, you can see that this extra margin is going to add a significant amount to its bottom line in the coming quarters. Best of all, there is no real execution risk associated with this growth. It's just a matter of waiting for its swaps portfolio to roll over.

Private Bank Buildout Continues

Aside from its NIM, the ongoing buildout of its private bank was another reason I was bullish on Citizens. By its nature, private banking tends to be relatively capital light (Citizens disclosed a 25% return on equity from this business in Q1), making it an attractive avenue for banks to explore. Of course, it takes time to scale up a new bank from scratch, so this endeavour was previously a drag on Citizens' earnings.

A year ago, this business only kicked in $0.04 to Citizens' quarterly EPS. With loans, deposits, and client assets all up strongly in that time, its contribution has risen to $0.11 as of last quarter. This remains modest in the grand scheme of things (Citizens earned $1.13 per share in Q1), but the direction of travel is positive.

Credit Quality Holding Up

Since my last piece, we have obviously seen significant uncertainty come from the Iran war and fears surrounding the health of private credit. Citizens doesn't appear to posting any concerning credit trends right now. Net charge-offs were less than 40 basis points of total loans last quarter, down both year-on-year and quarter, and balances of nonaccrual loans performed similarly. Positively, management is guiding for net charge-offs to be "stable to down slightly" in Q2, implying that credit conditions remain benign.

Shares Still Attractively Valued

Following last quarter's print, Citizens has now earned $4.23 per share in adjusted trailing 12-month net income. With the shares trading for $65.23 as I type, this leaves them on a trailing P/E multiple of 15.4x (the chart below has not updated for Q1 results, which is why it shows a trailing P/E of 16.84). For reference, Citizens; ten-year historical average trailing multiple is roughly 12.

Of course, the "E" part of this equation is still based on relatively depressed figures, as Citizens' average NIM across the last four quarters was only 3.03%. It was 3.14% last quarter, it's going to expand by another ~15-35 basis points through 2027. Similarly, the private banking unit is going to be earning more in the coming quarters than it did over the trailing four quarter period, contributing to higher earnings.

With that, analysts expect Citizens to earn $5.18 per share this year, before rising another 23% in 2027 to over $6.30 per share. I repeat that much of this growth is simply a function of the repricing of Citizens' balance sheet, involving little-to-no execution risk. On a trailing multiple of 12x, in line with its ten-year average, that would put Citizens at over $75 in a two years' time. Tack on $4 per share in expected dividend payments, and today's buyer can credibly expect a circa $80 total return price, mapping to a circa 11% annualized return. With much of its earnings growth requiring no real execution from management, and with credit quality holding up nicely, I view that as attractive. Accordingly, I am leaving my "Buy" rating for Citizens Financial Group in place.

data.title: Tesla: The Stock Market's Valuation Laws Don't Apply Here (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) analysis, the stock price had declined from about $400 per share to about $350 per share before now rising back to around $400. Ultimately $350 was a good entry point, and $400 is fine if one is viewing this as a long-term investment and not a short-term tactical trade. That said, there are several operational developments that have emerged since that analysis, and the time value of a $400 per share stock price has increased. I will provide these updates in this piece and explain why I remain moderately bullish.

The image for this analysis is an artistic depiction of a Chinese fire horse, which is a metaphor for Tesla stock, which is treated as mythic while breaking the normal laws of valuation the way the horse appears to outrun gravity itself.

The Current Landscape

Firstly, I will focus in on Tesla's enormous latent industrial leverage. The company's installed annual capacity spans California with over 550,000 Model 3/Ys, Shanghai at over 950,000, Berlin at over 375,000, and Texas at over 250,000. This is against deliveries of just 1.64M. Management also says that early Robotaxi operations can lean on an existing network that increased Supercharging stalls by over 3,800 in 2025. In other words, there's an already-built physical ecosystem here ready to be brought into the AI and autonomy generation.

Admittedly, the robotaxi operating model is still early, but it's not theoretical. Driverless robotaxis began testing in Austin in December, and the safety monitor was removed from some customer rides in January. The Bay Area service is still safety-driver only, but several additional metros have been mapped for the first half of 2026.

Ultimately, it's Tesla's AI stack that will differentiate it in the autonomous era. Bears look at Tesla and say it's just a car company, but anyone who's gone deep into tech analysis understands that, from first principles, Tesla is an AI company. To be more specific, I feel comfortable calling it a robotics company shifting from a legacy automobile model. It is also diversified via its energy storage and generation segment, which rose 27% year-over-year in 2025.

Tesla uses vision-based systems and in-house inference chips to train its neural nets for Full Self-Driving and for its robotics. With v14 training on both customer and robotaxi data, the fleet can gain over 500 years of continuous driving data per day. Active Full Self-Driving subscriptions reached 1.1M in 2025, so the flywheel is healthy and continues to evolve. This is a remarkable data-intelligence moat.

Diving a bit deeper into the AI leadership position, Tesla's Cortex 1 AI supercomputer cluster in Texas is operating >100,000 H100s, Cortex 2 is under construction, and onsite compute should more than double in the first half of 2026. Tesla's AI5 chip is also targeting a 50x performance gain against AI4 and is planned for production in 2027. Moreover, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF) is planning to manufacture advanced AI semiconductors in the U.S., which significantly reduces geopolitical risks for Tesla.

Valuation Laws Don't Apply To Tesla

If you don't like speculation, you probably won't buy Tesla stock. That said, I've self-trained religiously in the value investing school of thought, including all traditional variants of discounted value of future growth, peer-relative and sector-relative multiple analysis, and other methods of fundamental valuation estimation. All of that has to be understood, but once it is, you can throw it out the window in favor of making cash. In my opinion, an elite trader moves from asking, “What is this company worth today?” to “What will this company be priced at in X amount of time?” If you think the latter negates fundamental valuation analysis, you aren't doing it properly. But you have to break the rules.

As the famous saying goes:

“Learn the rules like a pro, so you can break them like an artist.” — Pablo Picasso.

Let's come back to the basics. Tesla has a forward P/E non-GAAP ratio that is 1,120% higher than the sector median and a forward PEG non-GAAP ratio that is 250% higher than the sector median. On the surface, you might reject a stock trading like that out of principle. I wouldn't blame you, but it doesn't mean it won't still make you money in the long term. Some stocks defy valuation laws and get away with it. Tesla is one such stock (and it's been doing it for many years). In fact, if you bought Tesla 10 years ago, your total return would be 2,300% today. How's that for speculation?

The only thing I'd be cautious of is that if you look at the long-term 10-year average in the P/S ratio chart above, the current price is substantially higher. That doesn't mean that it's not apt to invest in Tesla at this juncture, but it does mean that the company's valuation has no margin of safety versus its own historical average in buyer sentiment.

The caveat here is that the company's forward EPS long-term growth CAGR consensus estimate is about 37% annually, while its five-year average is 28.5%, meaning that there is a 30% increase in forward growth versus historical. This warrants an increase in the valuation multiples, but I find it unlikely that over a 50% rise in Tesla's P/S multiple against its long-term historical average is warranted as a result. In other words, the premium on Tesla stock right now makes it moderately overvalued even when using a nuanced valuation method that appreciates and acknowledges Tesla's long-term capability to defy the stock market's valuation laws.

I'm going to price in compression here to my forward estimate and forecast April 2028 revenue of $150B with a forward P/S in April 2027 of 12.5. If total shares outstanding rise over the next 12 months to ~4.1 billion (my estimate), then the stock price in 12 months will be about $460. The current price is $390, so the implied upside is 17%. If bought at the recent low of $350, the return would be 30%. As the long-term trajectory is strong, even with heavy overvaluation, I am reinstating my Buy rating here.

Risks On The Horizon

Waymo has over 500,000 fully autonomous robotaxi trips per week now, while already hitting over 20M lifetime rides. It's not the only competitor on the block, either, as Baidu, Inc. (BIDU) says Apollo Go delivered 3.4M fully driverless rides in Q4 alone while reaching a total of 26 cities (China speed helps). Tesla has the richer equity narrative and forward vision; there's no doubt in my mind about that, but rivals have stronger disclosed commercial robotaxi scale.

Energy is the ballast that can not only fund more of the autonomy and robotics ambitions Tesla has but also directly fuel autonomy and robotics scaling through better energy access. This is a dual win if the segment is developed strategically, and it is why I think it makes sense for energy to become a core segment for Tesla at this juncture during the pivot. In 2025, energy deployments rose 49% to 46.7 GWh, while energy revenue hit $12.8B and segment gross margin hit 29.8%, versus just 17.8% for the automotive gross margin.

The energy segment can help with the fact that BYD Company Limited (BYDDF) is a hard operating counterweight to TSLA sentiment on the EV front. BYD sold 2.26M EVs in 2025 versus Tesla's 1.64M vehicle deliveries, and in Germany, BYD registrations rose 327% in March, compared to 315% for Tesla. The price-war risk is not going to go away until Tesla has turned its autonomy moat into its primary operating advantage and real return driver.

Q1 Earnings Preview

Tesla reports on April 22 after the market closes, and with the stock trading high above averages, outlining operationally and fundamentally how the bridge from the current EV business to autonomy, AI, and robotics is developing will be key. This is far more important than beating current expectations, though that always helps. It's likely important for the investor base to understand, from simple first principles, where in the tech stack Tesla's long-term moats and distinguished competitive advantages are being established, as this is really what will sustain growth rates into the future and keep sentiment elevated.

Looking at near-term fundamentals, to hit Tesla's company-compiled 2026 delivery consensus of 1.69M after 358,023 units delivered in Q1, Tesla will need about 444,000 deliveries in each of the next three quarters. Hitting this target will come down to whether Q1 improvement in China and March strength in Germany are durable enough to meet expectations.

My final watch point is that Tesla ended 2025 with $44.1B of cash and investments and $6.2B of free cash flow. However, it also stated that 2026 includes six new production lines (Cybercab, Semi, and Megapack 3 are on schedule for volume production). However, if spending ramps faster than monetization, the bull case will become more duration-sensitive quickly, though I would view any pullback in this case as a long-term entry point.

Conclusion: Buy

Tesla stock is not for the faint of heart, but it is the kind of investment I would want to own for the long term. Ironically, Tesla is a kind of set-it-and-forget-it investment at this juncture, because the autonomy trajectory is massive, and trading in and out of cycles like I do is better done in other stocks. I don't own it, though, primarily because I maximize my annual return via rotation.

The price could be better, I admit, but you get what you pay for. Whether you like it or not, when you buy Tesla, you aren't just buying a company; you're buying a myth. If you don't like that, then step aside. You'll find plenty of traditional value stocks in other corners of the market. Tesla rewards the courageous and those who know how to think differently. I'm staying bullish, but I'm personally not buying unless I find an even better entry point ahead.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: America's Car-Mart's Crash Doesn't Justify An Upgrade At This Time
data.description:
It has been a rough couple of years for shareholders of America's Car-Mart, Inc. (CRMT). Back in June of 2024, I made the decision to downgrade the stock from a ‘hold’ to a ‘sell.’ This decision was not made lightly. After all, I hate taking a bearish stance on any business because it means that people, many of them good people, could get hurt if I am right. However, it is necessary when the fundamentals warrant it; otherwise, you are just deluding yourself. But I digress. Since I made that decision, the company has actually performed even worse than I anticipated. As of the time of this writing, the stock is down 79%. That compares to the 19.6% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. The good news is that management is taking steps in order to cut costs. But honestly, I don't see any reason to turn bullish or even neutral on it just yet. Until we can see signs of revenue stabilizing on a consistent basis or see a meaningful improvement in cash flow, I believe that maintaining a bearish outlook on the company is appropriate.

A Really Tough Ride

From an operational standpoint, I do think that America's Car-Mart is an interesting business. In case you are not terribly familiar with the company, it operates as one of the largest publicly held automotive retailers in the US. In fact, it currently has 136 dealerships nationwide. This is a space that I have a lot of experience in and that I like a great deal. But unfortunately, financial performance for the business has been challenged.

Some of this might seem surprising. I say that because there are certain things about the structure of the company that could play well to establishing a long-term competitive position. First and foremost, the company focuses on selling basic and affordable transportation for its customers, as opposed to many automotive retailers that sell high-priced vehicles. In the most recent quarter, for instance, the company, which sells used vehicles, recorded an average retail sales price of $20,634. That's cheap even by used vehicle standards. According to one source, the average price of used vehicles in the US stands at about $25,287. That same source says that, for a new vehicle, it's around $49,100.

Another positive about the company is that it typically operates in towns with populations of 50,000 or less. This means that, to the extent that there is not significant additional competition, the firm is able to establish itself as an important player in the automotive retail space in the areas in which it operates. Of course, this can be a double-edged sword. That's because, when new locations open up in those towns that might compete with America's Car-Mart, you end up with more challenging conditions than if the firm operated in more heavily populated areas.

Unfortunately, in recent years, financial performance for the company has been challenged. From 2023 through 2025, revenue has fallen slightly from $1.40 billion to $1.39 billion. Certain profitability metrics have worsened as well, though some have also improved. Take net profits as an example. Those declined from $20.4 million to $17.9 million. On the other hand, operating cash flow went from negative $135.7 million to negative $48.8 million. And at the same time that this occurred, EBITDA for the business expanded from $70.1 million to $101.4 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the first nine months of the 2026 fiscal year compared to the same time in 2025. In this case, most profitability metrics for the company followed revenue lower. The weakness that the company has seen here has been the result of challenging market conditions. As consumers become increasingly under pressure, and as a combination of inflationary pressures and tariffs has pushed up the cost of vehicles, the end result has been less buying activity. When it comes to tariffs specifically, management said that the impact on the automotive industry amounted to roughly $560 per vehicle when it came to procurement costs alone. This doesn't even factor in other impacts that could be rippling through the space.

From 2023 through 2025, the number of vehicles sold by the business dropped from 63,584 to 57,022. Some of this was because of a reduction in the number of locations that the business had, from 155 to 154. But overwhelmingly, it was the result of a reduction in the number of units sold per retail outlet. That number fell from 34.2 to 30.9. And for the first nine months of this year, the 27.4 sold was down from the 29.7 sold, and when combined with the decrease in the number of stores in operation from 154 to 136, it's not hard to see that the drop in the number of retail units from 41,373 to 37,480 makes sense.

Unfortunately, on a same-store basis, revenue for the company has been falling consistently since 2024. However, one thing that management has done in order to improve financial performance compared to what it would be otherwise has been to increase prices enough to boost profit margins. While the average retail price per used vehicle has risen, the chart below that illustrates that the gross profit and gross profit margin of those vehicles increased also.

Unfortunately for investors, there are signs of worsening market conditions. The auto loan delinquency rate recently came in at 5.2%. You would have to go back to the final quarter of 2010 to see that number come in that high. What's more, management is reporting that the number of accounts that they have that are 30 days or more past due increased to 4.4%. That compares to the 3.7% that we see when looking at the previous year, and it's up from the 3.1% achieved for 2024. This is not surprising when you consider how high the average interest rate on car loans that it facilitates is. The weighted average rate, according to management, is 17.66%. That is astronomical. With the consumer becoming increasingly stressed, with consumer confidence levels near all-time lows, this is an untenable interest rate for many Americans.

Those who are bullish about the company might point out that the stock is trading cheaper now than it probably ever has from a valuation standpoint. Take, if you will, the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples of the enterprise. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean here. The firm is trading at just a fraction of book value and tangible book value. This means that, in theory, if you were to liquidate the business and achieve fair market value that matches book value, you would significantly improve your overall net worth in the firm. But there are some issues with this approach.

The biggest one is that the vast majority of its assets are tied up in finance receivables and inventory. Upon liquidation, especially if it is done in a hurry, those would likely sell for fractions of a dollar. Even if we were to see a 39.4% haircut on the combination of these assets, that could potentially completely wipe out shareholder equity. And with market conditions worsening, such a haircut would not be unfathomable. It doesn't help that the company has net debt of $655.2 million, which is well above the $101.4 million of EBITDA that the business generated last year. Of course, most of the debt is nonrecourse in nature. But all this means is that it is backed by the finance receivables that the business has on its books. If the debt goes unpaid, those finance receivables are used as collateral.

In order to improve operations, it is true that management is working on cost-cutting initiatives. In fact, management closed what it called its Phase 2 stores in the middle of January of this year. Phase 2 specifically refers to the second phase of its cost-cutting initiative. That's why the number of locations of the business in operation plummeted so much from the end of 2025 to the end of the third quarter of 2026. Management does expect there to be a financial benefit from the firm's consolidation activities that will be reflected in the final quarter of the year, before ultimately flowing through to the profit and loss statement in subsequent quarters as well. But until we have some idea of the long-term impact of this, plus we see stabilization from a sales standpoint, I believe that caution is warranted.

Takeaway

The way I see things, it is entirely possible that America's Car-Mart might turn around and see some impressive upside. But at the same time, there's a lot of risk here. The way that the economy is, combined with the weakness that the company has exhibited in recent years, is not confidence-inducing. So even though there might be some potential, my view is that maintaining it as a ‘sell’ makes the most sense.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Month Gold Broke: 5 Lessons From The 'March Madness' Selloff And The Rebound Opportunity
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA and Jonathan Flynn

Introduction: The Golden Illusion

For decades, investors have treated gold as the ultimate financial anchor, the potential "safe haven" that holds steady when the rest of the world is in flames. However, the behavior of gold’s price in March 2026 provided a brutal reality check that challenged this long-held assumption. In a move that caught many off guard, gold plummeted 12% to finish the month at US$4,608/oz.1

This wasn’t just a minor correction; it was the metal's weakest monthly performance since June 2013.2 While gold remains up for the year, the suddenness of the drop left many wondering how an asset designed to protect against volatility could suddenly become a primary source of it. The answer lies not in a sudden loss of faith in gold’s value, but in the cold, hard mechanics of global liquidity.

It Wasn’t the News—It Was the Exit Door

When markets panic, the first thing investors reach for isn’t necessarily the safest asset but the most liquid one. The March 2026 drawdown in gold’s price was a classic example of "deleveraging" taking precedence over long-term fundamentals.

The data paints a starker picture than the headlines suggest. Global gold exchange-traded products shed 84 tons (US$12bn) in March 2026, but the pain was geographically concentrated. North America led the exodus with a massive 87-ton outflow (US$14bn), while Europe lost 7 tons. In a fascinating "East-West" divide, Asia saw a welcome positive inflow of 10 tons (US$1.9bn), as regional investors viewed the carnage as a buying opportunity.3

Under the hood, the "Momentum factors" were the primary culprits. Commodity Trading Advisors (CTAs) were positioned very long heading into mid-March before sharply unwinding those positions as the price trend reversed.4 While COMEX managed money net longs dropped by US$2bn (19 tons), the underlying signal for bulls is that the net long bias remains solid.5 The big players were forced to the exit door for liquidity, but they haven't abandoned the room entirely.

The Central Bank Plot Twist

The real wildcard emerged from Ankara. The Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey, a stalwart gold purchaser since 2017, suddenly became a significant source of supply. Turkey’s central bank utilized approximately 50 tons of gold as collateral, predominantly via swaps, to manage intense domestic currency pressure.6

This wasn't a change in long-term strategy but a tactical liquidity-driven move. It aligns with a broader global trend; the US Federal Reserve recently suggested that central banks were increasing their sales of US Treasuries to buffer against higher energy price risks.7 In a world of tightening liquidity, even the most dedicated central bank holders of gold could use their gold reserves as a tactical chip when their national currency is under fire.

The Bond Market Shock and "The Dollar Factor"

To understand the anatomy of this fall in the price of gold, we must look at the opportunity cost of holding gold. According to the Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM), Momentum was the single largest contributor to the negative return in March. However, the model also highlighted a startling "12% cumulative negative residual", which was a portion of the move that traditional macro factors simply couldn't explain.8

What we can explain is the bond market shock. The 10-year Treasury Inflation-Protected Security (TIPS) yield experienced a sharp jump, driven by rising nominal yields.9 When yields rise this quickly, gold is left with nowhere to hide. Gold returns are historically sensitive to real rates; as the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding asset skyrocketed alongside a strengthening US dollar, the speed of the inflation shock accelerated the price reversal beyond what the fundamentals predicted.

Why the Middle East Didn't Move the Needle

Standard market wisdom suggests that regional conflict should provide an automatic tailwind for gold. March debunked this, proving that geopolitical risk is often secondary to global liquidity needs.

Disruptions in parts of the Middle East failed to move the global needle. In Dubai, a "no flights, no tourists" trend significantly weighed on local demand for jewelry and small bars.10 This lack of physical traffic resulted in a local discount in gold prices compared to COMEX futures, highlighting a sharp disconnect between physical and paper markets. Trading volumes in Dubai increased, but the flow was insufficient to offset the massive deleveraging occurring in Western financial hubs.11

The ‘Green Shoots’ of Stabilization

Despite the historic sell-off, we believe the market is beginning to find its footing. These "green shoots" suggest the so-called "March Madness" may have been a transitory liquidity event:

  • The dollar’s ceiling: The US dollar is struggling to sustain gains and has failed to push beyond recent highs, reducing a primary pressure point.
  • Exchange-traded product reversal: Early April has seen positive Exchange-traded product inflows across regions, suggesting the mass exodus of March has paused.12
  • Policy rhetoric: Emerging macro signals suggest that further policy tightening in the US is likely to be more rhetorical than realized, providing a floor for gold.13
  • Constructive bias: Options markets show a constructive bias over a medium-term horizon, as seen in the 6-month risk reversals.14
  • Physical Support: Retail and physical demand are appearing as the price stabilizes above key technical levels.

History Shows These Selloffs Create Windows of Opportunity for Both Gold and Gold Miners

Where we believe things get interesting is in reviewing what has tended to happen, historically, after pullbacks in gold’s price performance. We recognize that past performance does not tell us exactly what the future will bring, but the historical record is still informative.

In the analysis that follows, we use the following:

  • SPDR Gold Shares (GLD): This is the most widely followed exchange-traded product designed to deliver the return, minus fees and expenses, of holding physical gold in a vault. It is the largest by assets under management as of this writing.
  • VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX): This is the most widely followed exchange-traded product meant to deliver the return, minus fees and expenses, of holding gold mining equities. Its underlying index is the MarketVector™ Global Gold Miners Index.

Figure 1a indicates:

  • We have defined 3 distinct types of ‘drawdown’ when looking at GLD’s performance, which are a 10% pullback, a 20% pullback, and then what we call a ‘Shock trough’ which has been defined by a 20% pullback coupled with macroeconomic stress.
  • The critical question, once these periods are defined, is what has the gold price, shown as GLD, and the return of gold mining equities, shown as GDX, been after these moves. In Figure 1a, we look at periods 6-month and 12-month after the pullback, and we show the range of returns, meaning we indicate the worst and the best return value for each period and note that the majority of observations was between those values. None of this guarantees what may transpire in the future, but it is good to have this type of historical context for analysis.

Figure 1a: Drawdowns as Opportunity: Historical Returns for GLD and GDX

Source: Morningstar, with data accessed through WisdomTree’s Fund Compare system, with data reviewed since GLD’s inception, 11/18/2004 to denote the different types of pullbacks. Returns are shown in net asset value (NAV) terms. Each band of returns represents the high value and low value observed for each type of period, meaning for all the periods where GLD had a 10% pullback, the GLD return 6 months after drawdown saw a range of results between +6% and +10%. Other cells in the table can be interpreted analogously. Past performance is not indicative of future returns. Standardized performance for GLD and GDX is shown in Figure 2b.

Figure 1b looks at similar data points, but instead of asking, ‘what was the average experience after a certain pullback?’, we define certain known ‘tough periods’ and we look at the returns of GLD and GDX during certain periods of time after those known troughs.

  • The pattern that we saw in Figure 1a seems to hold, in that there are the known ‘trough’ periods where GLD’s performance experiences a drawdown of some magnitude. From the trough, the GLD return 6 and 12 months looked positive, and GDX’s return over those same periods also looked positive, but in a greater magnitude.

Figure 1b: GLD and GDX After Major Drawdowns: Evidence from Key Market Events

Returns are shown in net asset value (NAV) terms. Past performance is not indicative of future returns. Standardized performance for GLD and GDX is shown in Figure 2b.

Introducing Capital Efficient Exposure to Gold

One of the historical issues that we have seen investors have with exposure to gold or gold miners regards how to add it to an overall allocation. Adding asset classes after trough periods is not easy to do. Holding a ‘gold position’ in a portfolio is open to a number of critiques, including how the metal has no earnings and no dividends, and the miners have had long periods of lackluster returns. WisdomTree has developed two distinct strategies that seek to mitigate these issues:

  • WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund (GDMN): This strategy is designed to provide a 90% exposure to the equities of gold miners, 90% exposure to gold futures, and 10% exposure to short-term U.S. treasury securities to serve as collateral for the futures. It thereby removes the need to think about whether an allocation is to gold or gold miners and creates an avenue to gain this exposure to both within one strategy.
  • WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Equity Strategy Fund (GDE): This strategy is designed to provide a 90% exposure to 500 large market capitalization U.S. equities, weighted by market cap, 90% exposure to gold futures, and 10% exposure to short-term U.S. treasury securities to serve as collateral for the futures. Instead of needing to draw down a U.S. equity position to make room for a gold allocation, one can make one allocation that has exposure to both.

Figure 2a shows the live performance history of GDE and GDMN and how they have compared to GLD and GDX, which were mentioned earlier. Due to limited live track records, we couldn’t include them in the Figure 1a and 1b analyses.

GDMN has meaningfully outpaced GDX and GLD across all measured horizons, reinforcing that this leveraged exposure to underlying gold prices has been beneficial. The divergence is most pronounced over the past year, where miners delivered substantially higher returns, but the pattern persists over longer periods as well. Even year-to-date, GDMN is leading, albeit by a smaller margin. This suggests that improving margins, operational leverage, and investor positioning are amplifying gains, positioning gold miners as a higher-beta expression of the broader gold trend. For people with bullish theses on both gold and gold miners should review the structure and potential of GDMN, in our opinion.

Figure 2a: Gold Miners Lead the Rally

Figure 2b: Standardized Returns

Sources: Morningstar, FactSet and WisdomTree, specifically data is from the PATH Fund Comparison Tool, accessed as of April 9, 2026, but showing returns for the period ended April 8, 2026 for Figure 2a and March 31, 2026 for 2b. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

It’s clear that GDMN has an implicit double exposure to gold, which can come with volatility, layering together both the returns of gold futures and the returns of gold miners. GDE, on the other hand, is more geared towards asset allocation. A broad-based U.S. equity exposure alongside a gold futures exposure could be more likely to see lower internal correlation of returns. A reasonable hypothesis may be that GDMN should tend toward higher relative volatility, whereas GDE should tend toward lower relative volatility. We look at 1-Year rolling volatility figures in Figure 3. It is interesting how GLD and GDE's rolling 1-Year volatility measures are similar over much of the available time series, even though GDE has implicit leverage and GLD does not. This may not always be the case, but it is speaking to the lower correlation between broad U.S. equities and the returns of gold futures.

Sources: Morningstar, FactSet and WisdomTree, specifically data is from the PATH Fund Comparison Tool, accessed as of April 9, 2026, but showing 1-Year rolling volatility of returns for the period ended March 31, 2026. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Conclusion: Liquidity Needs Can Break Gold Price Rallies—But They Also Create Historical Opportunities

March was not a failure of gold’s role, but a reminder of how markets function under stress. Liquidity, not fundamentals, dictated price. Yet history suggests these moments rarely mark the end of the story—they create the setup. As flows stabilize, real rates peak, and positioning resets, gold and gold equities often recover meaningfully. For investors, the takeaway is clear: the same forces that drove the sharp selloff may now be laying the foundation for the next leg higher.

GDMN: The Fund is actively managed and invests in U.S.-listed gold futures and global equity securities issued by companies that derive at least 50% of their revenue from the gold mining business (“Gold Miners”). The Fund’s use of U.S.-listed gold futures contracts will give rise to leverage, magnifying gains and losses and causing the Fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged. Moreover, the price movements in gold and gold futures contracts may fluctuate quickly and dramatically, and have a historically low correlation with the returns of the stock and bond markets. By investing in the equity securities of Gold Miners, the Fund may be susceptible to financial, economic, political, or market events that impact the gold mining sub-industry, including commodity prices and the success of exploration projects. The Fund may invest a significant portion of its assets in the securities of companies of a single country or region, including emerging markets, and thus, the Fund is more likely to be impacted by events and political, economic, or regulatory conditions affecting that country or region, or emerging markets generally. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund’s investment process is heavily dependent on quantitative models and the models may not perform as intended.

GDE: The Fund is actively managed and invests in U.S. listed gold futures and U.S. equity securities. The Fund’s use of U.S. listed gold futures contracts will give rise to leverage, magnifying gains and losses and causing the Fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged. Moreover, the price movements in gold and gold futures contracts may fluctuate quickly and dramatically, and have a historically low correlation with the returns of the stock and bond markets. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund's investment process is heavily dependent on quantitative models and the models may not perform as intended.
